claim: Whole-repo indexing before query time is the architectural prerequisite for any deep value extraction — both Greptile and Sourcegraph Cody reject the analyze-the-diff model in favor of precomputed cross-repo context
evidence: Greptile builds a three-node dependency graph queried at review time, not the diff. Sourcegraph uses SCIP symbol indexing enabling compiler-accurate cross-repo Find References. Both documented in official sources.

claim: Greptile and Sourcegraph Cody catch problem classes that static analysis fundamentally cannot: cross-file architectural drift, business logic consistency, and intent-aware errors
evidence: Static analyzers operate file-by-file and apply predefined rules. They cannot correlate frontend/backend defaults, detect API removal breaking auth flows elsewhere, or surface naming inconsistencies across files. Both tools maintain holistic codebase context through indexing rather than analyzing files in isolation.

claim: Augment Code's finding that a weaker model with great context (Sonnet + MCP) outperforms a stronger model with poor context (Opus without MCP) is the central empirical validation of context quality as the primary lever
evidence: +80% quality improvement for Claude Code + Opus 4.5 with Context Engine MCP vs without. +71% improvement for Cursor + Claude Opus 4.5. Benchmark on 300 Elasticsearch PRs, 900 attempts.

claim: Sourcegraph deprecated vector embeddings at Enterprise GA, replacing them with BM25F + transformer reranker, citing ~20% improvement across all key metrics
evidence: Official Sourcegraph blog on BM25F. Complexity of maintaining vector DBs at >100K repos scale was cited as the reason for deprecation. BM25F is recall-optimized first stage; transformer reranker is precision-optimized second stage.

claim: Repomix (22.8k GitHub stars, ~45,500 weekly npm downloads) is the dominant CLI tool for full-text repo flattening with four output formats, Tree-sitter compression, Secretlint secret detection, MCP server mode, and a full Node.js programmatic API
evidence: Official Repomix GitHub repository and documentation verified. Renamed from Repopack December 2024.

claim: Full-text flattening wins for holistic reasoning; RAG wins for scale and precision on targeted queries. Best practice 2025/2026: use full-text for repos under 200K tokens; use RAG for anything larger or for production Q&A systems
evidence: RAG is 1,250x lower cost per query but introduces retrieval lottery risk. 200K token threshold derived from Claude context window. Supported by elastic.co/search-labs and promptmetheus.com.

claim: Repomix --compress flag uses Tree-sitter parsing to yield approximately 70% token reduction by keeping function signatures and discarding implementations. Significant reduction is HIGH confidence; the specific 70% figure has LOW confidence.
evidence: 70% figure appears in Repomix MCP server docs but compression guide marks feature as experimental and does not publish specific percentages. Multiple secondary sources cite 70%.

claim: Questions that work well with repo-to-text: architecture review, onboarding, cross-cutting refactoring, documentation generation, security audits, migration planning. Questions that fail: exact debugging of runtime behavior, tasks requiring post-training-cutoff library knowledge, granular debugging on large repos
evidence: Based on practitioner evidence from Addy Osmani, Simon Willison, Peter Steinberger. Repomix use-cases documentation.

claim: NASA RRL provides a 9-dimension scoring ladder (Documentation, Extensibility, IP, Modularity, Packaging, Portability, Standards Compliance, Support, Verification/Testing) scored 1-9 per dimension. RRL 1-2 = not ready; RRL 8-9 = demonstrated multi-organization reuse
evidence: NASA ESDS Software Reuse Working Group. RRL finalized 2010. Secondary sources confirmed 9 dimensions and 1-9 scale.

claim: CHAOSS OSS Viability model is the most operationally complete framework for evaluating open source components for adoption. Key thresholds: Libyears > 3 years signals elevated vulnerability risk; Elephant Factor = 1 signals existential single-company dependency risk
evidence: CHAOSS official practitioner guide. Libyears claim: outdated deps 4x more likely to have publicly disclosed vulnerabilities. Elephant Factor = minimum number of orgs comprising 50% of activity.

claim: Garlan, Allen & Ockerbloom's Architectural Mismatch (1995, revisited 2009) identifies four assumption categories that cause reuse failure: nature of components, nature of connectors, global architectural structure, construction process. API compatibility checking misses all four.
evidence: IEEE Software 1995 and 2009 retrospective confirmed that architectural mismatch remains a leading cause of reuse failure despite dramatic changes in software systems.

claim: Empirical evidence strongly supports reuse improving quality (lower defect density) and productivity. Reused components show ~20% total effort reduction from defect density savings. A 2023 model explained 58-66% of variance in productivity and bug density outcomes.
evidence: 2024 SLR (ScienceDirect) and 2023 Springer empirical study. Multiple independent peer-reviewed sources.

claim: Code reuse initiative failure follows predictable patterns: NIH syndrome, funding model mismatch, framework over-engineering, and maintenance cost underestimation. The consistent prescription: start with concrete working systems and generalize, not with top-down reusable designs.
evidence: Schmidt (Vanderbilt) analysis and Morris practitioner analysis. Consistent across multiple independent sources.

claim: Approximately 70% of the orchestration infrastructure needed for a value-extraction skill already exists internally. The deep-research pipeline, gsd-codebase-mapper, explore agent, ecosystem-health scoring model, and TDMS schema conventions are all directly reusable or adaptable with low-medium effort.
evidence: D1d systematic audit of all internal agents/skills against value-extraction requirements. Confirmed by D5 delta analysis.

claim: Six capabilities must be built new: repo-discovery agent, portability-scoring agent, adaptation-guide-writer, value-findings JSONL schema + intake pipeline, external-repo domain config YAML, and summary vs. extraction mode switch
evidence: D1d and D5 systematically confirmed the absence of these capabilities in the existing agent/skill inventory.

claim: Seven GitHub REST search endpoints exist with authenticated limits of 30 req/min (10 req/min for code search) and a hard 1,000-result cap across REST and GraphQL. Date-range slicing is the validated workaround.
evidence: GitHub official REST API docs. GitHub Changelog 2023-03-10. PyGithub issues and community discussions confirmed the cap.

claim: GitHub Trending and Explore have NO official API — GitHub confirmed these are UI-only views citing abuse prevention, dynamic ranking logic, and CDN optimization. No announcement of future API plans.
evidence: GitHub community discussion #161519. Confirmed by absence of API endpoints.

claim: For finding actively maintained code, pushed: qualifier is more reliable than updated: qualifier. A composite repo quality validation scoring approach: combine pushed: recency + fork-to-star ratio > 0.1 + has CONTRIBUTING.md/CHANGELOG + CI/CD badge presence.
evidence: GitHub community discussions on pushed_at vs updated_at semantics. Quality validation scoring approach synthesized from D2d practitioner evidence.

claim: API and web interface search results are provably inconsistent — same query can return 225 results via API vs 123,000 via web. GitHub acknowledged code search API should be treated as best effort.
evidence: Multiple community discussions with documented discrepancies. GitHub community discussions #134904, #56494, #48703.

claim: The new GitHub code search uses Tree-sitter indexing with regex, boolean operators, and symbol search (symbol:functionName) across 21+ languages — architecturally separate from the legacy REST /search/code endpoint
evidence: GitHub code search syntax docs. GitHub Blog: Improving GitHub code search.

claim: npm search API is the most feature-rich among language registries. The npms.io API provides richer 0-10 scoring across three categories (quality 0.30, maintenance 0.35, popularity 0.35) with biweekly updates.
evidence: npm Registry API docs. npms-analyzer architecture.md (quality/maintenance/popularity weights confirmed). Academic paper (Simplifying the Search of npm Packages, 2020).

claim: PyPI has NO search API. The XML-RPC search() method was permanently decommissioned September 2024. PyPI's BigQuery dataset is the only way to run aggregate queries across the full corpus.
evidence: PyPI official discussion and warehouse issue tracker confirmed permanent decommissioning.

claim: Go modules use the module path as the source URL, providing near-100% source URL accuracy by design. pkg.go.dev has NO public search API (issue #36785 open since January 2020, milestone pkgsite/unplanned).
evidence: Official Go modules reference. GitHub issue #36785 confirmed milestone and no resolution expected.

claim: deps.dev (Google Open Source Insights) is the best cross-registry API, covering 7 ecosystems with computed transitive dependency graphs, OpenSSF Scorecard per package, OSV advisory integration, SLSA provenance attestations, and hash-based lookup. Free, no auth required.
evidence: deps.dev API v3 official docs. Security.googleblog.com announcement April 2023.

claim: libraries.io covers 31+ registries with dependent-count metadata not available in individual APIs. The bulk dataset on Zenodo (2020, stale) covers 2.5M components and 100M declared dependencies. Live API continues to update.
evidence: libraries.io API docs. Zenodo dataset record confirmed 2020 date. Platform count varies across sources (31-36).

claim: The awesome-lists ecosystem has ~9,924 repos on GitHub and is fully API-accessible via ecosyste.ms (5,000 req/hr, CC BY-SA 4.0). Quality is enforced by awesome-lint with ~33 checks.
evidence: GitHub topics:awesome count confirmed March 2026. ecosyste.ms official service with OpenAPI spec.

claim: The Algolia HN API provides full-text search over Hacker News with 10,000 req/hr, no auth required. For Show HN repo discovery: search_by_date?query=github.com&tags=show_hn&numericFilters=points>50. Average Show HN repo gains 121 stars in 24 hours, 289 after a week.
evidence: Official HN API (Firebase). Algolia HN search API. Academic preprint on HN impact (arxiv 2511.04453v1). Note: algolia/hn-search GitHub project archived 2025 but API appears operational.

claim: A cross-vector compound discovery strategy — repos appearing in multiple signals (awesome list + HN Show HN + TLDR mention) within a rolling 90-day window — is the highest-quality approach. Intersection across vectors dramatically reduces fake-star false positives.
evidence: Synthesized from D2c practical workflow tier analysis. ecosyste.ms API cross-references download/dependency data enabling multi-signal validation.

claim: Fake GitHub stars are a massive signal-pollution problem. A CMU study (ICSE 2026) detected 6 million suspected fake stars across 26,254 repos, with ~30% of repos with fake star campaigns being active spam/malware. Stars alone are now an unreliable quality signal.
evidence: arxiv.org/abs/2412.13459 (published late 2024). CMU news confirmation. 16% of repositories in 2024 dataset affected.

claim: The Bing Web Search API was fully retired August 11, 2025. The primary alternatives are: Brave Search API (independent 40B-page index, SOC 2 Type II), Serper.dev (Google SERP, $0.30-1.00/1k), Exa.ai (semantic, $1.50/1k), and GitHub's native search API.
evidence: Multiple sources confirm August 2025 retirement. Microsoft official documentation and Firecrawl blog on alternatives.

claim: Phind outperforms Perplexity for code-specific repo discovery (95% vs 80% in API documentation searches). Perplexity wins for broader research context queries.
evidence: Side-by-side comparisons from community blogs. Single third-party comparison source — not independently validated.

claim: Stack Overflow SEDE (weekly snapshot, free SQL) and BigQuery (quarterly update) enable SQL queries against all answer bodies for GitHub links. High-score accepted answers with GitHub links are especially high-signal discovery candidates.
evidence: SEDE official GitHub. Stack Exchange API docs. BigQuery public data confirmed.

claim: Robert Martin's package metrics provide the quantitative portability baseline. Ideal portable reusable components: A≈1, I≈0 (stable abstract packages). Zone of Pain (A≈0, I≈0): concrete and stable, hard to change. Zone of Uselessness (A≈1, I≈1): abstract but unstable.
evidence: Academic foundations from Martin's Agile Software Development (2002). NDepend docs. Wikipedia Software Package Metrics. Confirmed across D3a, D3b-2, D1c.

claim: A 2024 PLOS One ML study of 526 Java Maven artifacts found the #1 reusability predictor is PUA (Public Undocumented API) < 450. Documentation is the strongest single predictor, followed by Number of Files < 250, NII max < 80 per class, NL sum < 450, CBO std dev 2-8.
evidence: PMC/PLOS One article PMC11824963. Ground truth: token-based code clone detection, HIGH reuse = 21+ detections.

claim: Framework coupling vs. library coupling is the decisive architectural portability boundary. Hexagonal Architecture (ports and adapters) converts framework coupling to library coupling, maximizing portability of the application core.
evidence: Embedded Artistry field atlas. Herbert Graça explicit architecture blog. Dependency inversion Wikipedia. Consistent across multiple sources.

claim: COCOMO II's Software Understanding (SU) factor shows: a component with Low SU multiplies adaptation cost by 5x even for trivial changes. A NASA study of ~3,000 reused modules showed a base cost of ~5% just to assess, select, and assimilate a component, before any modification.
evidence: COCOMO II documentation. GeeksforGeeks secondary source. NASA study of ~3,000 reused modules.

claim: The blank project test is the most practically useful portability heuristic: list all imports of a component. Any import that is not standard library, a well-known general-purpose library, or behind an abstracted interface is a portability risk.
evidence: Synthesized across D3a sources from multiple practitioner and academic sources. Not from a single paper but convergent across multiple frameworks.

claim: A proposed 5-dimension portability rubric (scored 0-15): Dependency Profile, Coupling Profile, Configuration Surface, Cognitive Portability, Documentation Artifacts. Score >= 10 = strong portable candidate; 6-9 = conditional; < 6 = project-specific. Note: composite not empirically validated.
evidence: Each dimension independently evidence-backed (Martin metrics, PLOS One ML study, COCOMO II, Muratori, Twelve-Factor). Composite rubric is synthesized, not validated against adoption outcomes.

claim: The strongest signal for an extraction candidate is high internal cohesion AND high change frequency (Tornhill hotspot composite metric). High-churn, high-complexity components in isolation from the core domain are ideal extraction targets.
evidence: understandlegacycode.com analysis of Software Design X-Rays methodology. CodeScene behavioral analysis approach.

claim: Change coupling is a more reliable extraction signal than static dependency analysis alone — files that consistently co-change in the same commits but live in different modules empirically belong together.
evidence: understandlegacycode.com key points of Software Design X-Rays. codesai.com bundling-up post (2026).

claim: The Rule of Three governs extraction timing: extract when a pattern appears three times, not two. Even with three occurrences, do not extract if you cannot name the abstraction clearly or if it requires boolean parameters to handle variants.
evidence: understandlegacycode.com rule-of-three article. Fowler refactoring principles.

claim: Shopify's 7-step Strangler Fig process is production-validated. A 2025 insight adds: implement stranglers in outputs-first order (pure outputs → I/O combinations → inputs last) to avoid the Writeback anti-pattern.
evidence: Shopify engineering blog. Sherman on Software 2025-04-21 post on outputs-first ordering.

claim: DDD Bounded Contexts are the canonical service extraction unit. Signals: same term has different meanings in different areas (ubiquitous language divergence), business rules differ significantly, different teams own different areas.
evidence: Microsoft Azure Architecture Center (2026 update). Martin Fowler DDD canonical references.

claim: Micro-frontend extraction requires 4+ independent teams to justify the overhead. 2025 consensus: for most teams, a monolithic frontend is the right choice. Modular monolith is the recommended mid-ground.
evidence: Martin Fowler micro-frontends article (updated). dev.to 2025 consensus post. Consistent across multiple 2025 practitioner sources.

claim: bit.dev enables component-level extraction with versioning. Limitations: no free-tier self-hosting for private scopes, documentation inconsistencies, publishing workflow differs from git push.
evidence: bit.dev official docs. Community experience reports.

claim: git-filter-repo is the recommended tool for surgical repository splitting — ~20 seconds on large repos vs 12+ minutes for older tools. git subtree is slower but supports bidirectional syncing.
evidence: GitHub official docs on splitting a subfolder. git-filter-repo GitHub README. Community performance comparison.

claim: PNPM workspaces is the standard choice for extracting shared code within a monorepo. ~60-80% disk reduction vs npm/yarn. Does not solve cross-repo sharing — npm publish still required for external consumers.
evidence: pnpm official docs. Multiple community monorepo guides.

claim: Tier 1 detectable architectural patterns (high accuracy, high value): circular dependencies (dependency-cruiser/Madge), God Components (LOC + FanIn/FanOut), Hub-Like Dependencies, Hotspots (Code Maat: churn × complexity), and Temporal Coupling (git log co-change).
evidence: dependency-cruiser active GitHub repo. Arcan empirical validation on 103 Java projects (72M LOC). Code Maat open source. All confirmed as production-ready tools.

claim: LLMs achieve only 38% accuracy on design pattern classification (GoF), with GPT-4o and LLaMA-3 as top performers. LLMs are useful for description/explanation tasks but not for precise automated pattern detection — they hallucinate patterns confidently at a 62% false-positive rate.
evidence: arxiv.org/html/2501.04835v1: 11 models tested across 12 GoF patterns in Java and Python. Chalmers 2025 peer-reviewed study found RoBERTa at F1=0.91 for ML-based (not LLM) detection.

claim: Code Maat detects hotspots, logical coupling, ownership patterns from git history. The 2025 hotspot study validated line-level detection at 90% accuracy. Critical finding: 73.9% of hotspot commits are from bots — a repo analysis tool must filter bot commits before computing hotspots.
evidence: Code Maat GitHub. arxiv.org/html/2602.13170 (Source Code Hotspots, 2025). 90% accuracy with Cohen's Kappa 0.61 for inter-rater agreement.

claim: Higher-order architectural patterns (CQRS, hexagonal, event-driven) lack dedicated automated detection tools. Detection requires combining naming convention analysis, dependency direction analysis, import analysis, and structural heuristics. All LLM-identified patterns should be reported as detected pattern intent with confidence percentage.
evidence: D3c review of all tools found no equivalent of FINDER or Arcan for CQRS/hexagonal/event-driven. Consistent across all reviewed academic and tool surveys.

claim: Conway's Law alignment is measurable via CodeScene (git authorship + architectural boundary comparison). Misalignment predicts defect density and delivery slowdowns. A repo where team ownership boundaries do not align with module boundaries is a value extraction risk.
evidence: CodeScene architectural analyses documentation. CodeScene awarded Best Paper at International Conference on Technical Debt 2024.

claim: Structured dimension-by-dimension analysis beats open-ended exploration for analytical tasks (20-50% accuracy improvement). Open-ended exploration is specifically recommended for serendipitous discovery. Optimal approach: structured pass for known dimensions + open-ended pass for discovery in separate phases.
evidence: Anthropic Claude Code Best Practices official docs. Multiple practitioner sources. Convergent evidence across independent sources.

claim: Anthropic's own pattern is Explore → Plan → Implement: enter Plan Mode, read files and answer questions without making changes, then produce structured output. The explore.md agent in this codebase implements this exact 5-step pattern.
evidence: Anthropic Claude Code Best Practices official docs. How Anthropic Teams Use Claude Code official blog. Confirmed by local codebase explore.md agent.

claim: Context window is the binding constraint: performance degrades as context fills. Three anti-patterns: kitchen-sink sessions, unscoped investigate prompts reading hundreds of files, over-specified CLAUDE.md. Fix for large codebases: subagents — delegate investigation to separate context windows that report summaries back.
evidence: Anthropic Claude Code Best Practices: 'Most best practices are based on one constraint: Claude's context window fills up fast, and performance degrades as it fills.'

claim: Multi-pass analysis with phase separation produces significantly better results than single-pass. Canonical pattern: Structure pass → Pattern pass → Quality/Concerns pass. Each pass writes documents to disk rather than accumulating in context.
evidence: Convergent evidence from gsd-codebase-mapper.md (local), gist.github.com 6-phase framework, Code-Survey arxiv methodology. Confirmed by Anthropic best practices.

claim: Structured output via JSON Schema/JSONL reduces format errors by up to 70% and achieves up to 99% reliability with API-native structured outputs. JSONL with SHA-256 fingerprint-based dedup + severity/effort/confidence fields + file:line references is the battle-tested pattern.
evidence: agenta.ai structured outputs guide. API-native structured outputs documentation. Local AUDIT_TEMPLATE.md as ground truth.

claim: What's interesting here prompts require explicit permission and scaffolding: give permission to deviate from standard quality metrics, prime with categories of insight, use survey methodology (design questions first, then drill).
evidence: Anthropic Claude Code best practices endorses vague prompts for exploration. Code-Survey methodology (arxiv). kamilstanuch/codebase-digest 60+ prompt library. Effectiveness is anecdotal — no controlled studies.

claim: Claude Code uses a three-tier tool hierarchy: Glob (near-zero token cost), Grep (lightweight ripgrep), Read (expensive, reserved for confirmed-relevant files). Just-in-time retrieval — explore cheaply with Glob/Grep, then Read only confirmed-relevant files.
evidence: vadim.blog Claude Code analysis. code.claude.com/docs/en/how-claude-code-works official docs.

claim: Naively loading large repos into 1M token context windows degrades quality measurably — 17-point retrieval accuracy decline (93% to 76%) measured as context fills on Claude Opus 4.6 at 1M tokens. Token cost doubles beyond 200K ($3/M to $6/M).
evidence: Anthropic context engineering research (official blog). Augment comparison showing optimized 200K beats raw 1M dumps. factory.ai context window problem analysis.

claim: Four distinct context failure modes: Context Poisoning (errors compound), Context Distraction (agents repeat past actions past 100K tokens), Context Confusion (superfluous info degrades function-calling — every added tool hurts), Context Clash (sharded info causes 39% performance drop).
evidence: dbreunig.com/2025/06/22 how contexts fail. Stack Overflow Developer Survey 2025. Multiple supporting studies.

claim: Aider's PageRank approach on a tree-sitter dependency graph provides compressed structural orientation without vector infrastructure. Default 1,024 tokens. Advantage over RAG: deterministic, explicit dependency tracing. Limitation: misses semantic/conceptual relationships without explicit symbol references.
evidence: aider.chat official blog and docs. deepwiki.com/Aider-AI/aider analysis.

claim: JetBrains Research (2025, SWE-bench Verified) found observation masking outperforms LLM summarization for context management — 2.6% higher solve rates at 52% lower cost. The common wisdom that intelligent summarization preserves more useful context than simple masking is empirically wrong.
evidence: blog.jetbrains.com/research/2025/12 efficient context management. Peer research on 500 SWE-bench instances.

claim: Amazon Science (arXiv 2602.23368, Feb 2026) found keyword search achieves 90%+ of RAG-level performance for most coding tasks without a vector database, validating Claude Code's Grep-first approach as near-optimal for most tasks.
evidence: arxiv.org/html/2602.23368 Amazon Science paper February 2026. Confirms semantic search advantages persist for conceptual queries on massive codebases.

claim: The Claude Code skill/agent ecosystem has grown to 2,300+ skills, 770+ MCP servers, and 95+ curated marketplaces as of March 2026. everything-claude-code: 82,000+ GitHub stars. Anthropic donated Agent Skills specification to Linux Foundation December 2025; OpenAI adopted same format.
evidence: code.claude.com/docs/en/discover-plugins official docs. Medium analysis of everything-claude-code. Multiple ecosystem tracking sources.

claim: SoNash's architecture mirrors community consensus pattern but at depth that outpaces most public repos. Most public repos implement 2-3 layers; SoNash implements all layers plus cross-session state, hook warning logs, TDMS, propagation enforcement, pattern registries, multi-agent teams, and convergence loops.
evidence: D4c systematic comparison against awesome-claude-code, everything-claude-code, and 10+ other repos. No publicly documented project matches the full integration depth.

claim: GSD (23k GitHub stars, used at FAANG/Shopify/Google) is the closest public equivalent to SoNash's SWS — phase-based execution with fresh 200K-token context per phase, parallel wave execution, and atomic git commits. SoNash adds canonical artifact enforcement, hook integration, cross-doc dependency tracking.
evidence: github.com/gsd-build/get-shit-done official repo. dev.to GSD guide. Enterprise adoption documented.

claim: SoNash has unique capabilities with no public equivalent: TDMS full pipeline, propagation enforcement (3-layer), CANON artifact enforcement (hook-checks.json), T3 convergence loop doctrine, review-metrics.jsonl RECONCILE step.
evidence: D4c systematic search found no public projects implementing equivalent systems under any terminology.

claim: The ecosystem has capabilities SoNash lacks: autonomous multi-agent swarms (Loki Mode: 37 agents; Ruflo: 100+ agents with Byzantine fault-tolerant voting), cross-harness config portability (AGENTS.md), OS-level sandboxing (Trail of Bits), credential protection deny rules.
evidence: Loki Mode HN discussion. Ruflo GitHub. AGENTS.md standard documentation. Trail of Bits DeepWiki/official config.

claim: 24 CVEs identified in Claude Code ecosystem; 655 malicious skills detected in supply chain attacks. SoNash's internal-only skill system avoids this risk entirely — an implicit security advantage worth explicitly preserving.
evidence: D4c community audit findings. Supply chain attack risk documented in ecosystem research.

claim: The following internal capabilities transfer directly with no modification: scripts/health/lib/scoring.js (scoreMetric, computeGrade, sparkline, compositeScore, computeTrend), write-to-disk-first principle, 4-agent concurrency cap + staged-wave pattern, CRAAP+SIFT evaluation framework, MECE coverage verification.
evidence: D1d and D5 systematic codebase audit. All capabilities confirmed via direct file reads.

claim: The deep-research parallel searcher/synthesizer architecture is directly applicable to external repo analysis. The codebase search profile uses Read, Grep, Glob, Bash. The claims.jsonl routing schema can be extended with adoption_candidate, portability, effort_to_adapt flags without breaking existing consumers.
evidence: D5 delta analysis. Direct read of deep-research-searcher.md and deep-research-synthesizer.md.

claim: The explore agent requires removing 2 context blocks (~50 lines) to become a generic external repo investigator. gsd-codebase-mapper requires output path change and stack-detection generalization for non-JS repos. deep-research domain module requires one new external-repo.yaml (~50 lines).
evidence: D1d systematic adaptation cost analysis. Based on direct reads of agent definitions.

claim: The gsd-project-researcher Mode 3 (Comparison) output format is directly applicable for this pattern vs my current approach analysis, with addition of a Portability Assessment section.
evidence: D5 delta analysis. Direct read of gsd-project-researcher.md Mode 3.

claim: The code-reviewer 7-category framework is reusable for external repos by adding an ADOPT tier to CRITICAL/WARNING/SUGGESTION tiers, and replacing the 10 SoNash-specific pattern rules with domain-agnostic quality signals. The AICode category needs zero changes.
evidence: D1d adaptation cost analysis. Direct read of code-reviewer.md.

claim: The ecosystem-health interactive triage loop provides the UX model for presenting value-extraction findings. What needs to change: replace 13 internal health dimensions with value-extraction dimensions and remap actions from fix commands to adoption actions.
evidence: D5 delta analysis. Direct reads of ecosystem-health SKILL.md and REFERENCE.md.

claim: Six new capabilities have no internal precedent and must be built: repo-discovery agent (~200 lines), portability-scoring agent (~250 lines), adaptation-guide-writer agent (~150 lines), value-findings JSONL schema + intake pipeline (3-4 scripts ~100 lines each), external-repo domain YAML (~50 lines), summary vs extraction mode switch.
evidence: D1d and D5 systematic absence confirmation. Confirmed via negative search — no existing agents/skills implement these capabilities.

claim: The recommended value-extraction skill architecture is a 7-phase pipeline: Phase 0 (Discovery optional), Phase 1 (Broad Mapping via gsd-codebase-mapper x4 parallel), Phase 2 (Value Scan via deep-research-searcher x3-6), Phase 3 (Value Synthesis), Phase 4 (Portability Analysis new build), Phase 5 (Adaptation Recipe new build), Phase 6 (JSONL Intake new build), Phase 7 (Interactive Triage reuse ecosystem-health UX).
evidence: Synthesized from D5 architecture recommendation and confirmed against all Gap 1-4 findings.

claim: The recommended default entry point for value extraction is Survey Mode + Deep Scan. Quick Scan is the correct pre-flight (under 30 seconds, answers license/activity/stack compatibility). Temporal Scan is opt-in for hotspot and behavioral analysis. Extraction Mode is invoked after Survey Mode identifies a specific finding.
evidence: Synthesized from D1d architecture decisions, D3b-2 extraction heuristics, D4a prompting strategies, D5 recommended architecture.

claim: The 7-phase pipeline is parallelizable in Phase 1 (4 codebase-mapper agents) and Phase 2 (3-6 value-scan searcher agents). Phase 4-5 can be parallelized per finding. Total elapsed time estimate for Survey Mode + Deep Scan on a medium repo: 5-15 minutes.
evidence: Based on deep-research parallel agent timing precedents. No direct timing benchmark for this specific pipeline.
